subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 7. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your job is to talk, show, and sort real objects alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Sort a group of objects into two sets using one rule (for example red / not red).
Explain the sorting rule they used in their own words.
Understand that a set is a group of things that belong together.
Count how many objects are in each set and answer how-many questions.
Compare two sets and say which has more and which has fewer.
Notice that the same objects can be sorted in different ways.
Understand that a Venn diagram is a sorting circle: things that fit go inside, things that do not stay outside.
Sort objects into a one-circle Venn diagram, and take first steps with two overlapping circles (the middle means both).
A pile of small objects to sort: buttons, dates, stones, bottle caps, beads, or dry beans in mixed colours and sizes.

concepts.title: What Is a Set? (sorting by one rule)
concepts.explanation: A set is simply a group of things that belong together because they share something – they are all red, all round, all animals, or all toys. Sorting is the act of putting objects into sets. This is the foundation of the whole unit, so spend the most time here with real objects the child can touch and move. How to teach it: Tip a small pile of buttons onto a mat. Say, ‘Let us put all the red ones here and all the not-red ones here.’ Move the first two or three yourself, thinking out loud, then let the child…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Look at all these buttons. Let us sort them. Put the red buttons in this pile.” Child sorts. You: “Now, why do all these belong together?” Child: “They are all red!” You: “Yes! This is a set…

concepts.title: Counting and Comparing Sets (more / fewer)
concepts.explanation: Once objects are sorted into two sets, the natural next question is ‘How many are in each?’ and ‘Which set has more?’ This links the child's counting skills from earlier units to the new idea of comparing groups. How to teach it: After sorting, count each set aloud together, touching each object once, and say the total: ‘This set has 5. This set has 3.’ Then compare: ‘5 is more than 3, so the set of red buttons has more.’ For the surest comparison, line the two sets up and match one object from each into pairs.…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us count. How many red buttons?” Child and you: “One, two, three, four, five.” You: “And how many blue buttons?” Child: “One, two, three.” You: “Five and three. Which is more?” Child:…

concepts.title: The Venn Diagram — One Sorting Circle
concepts.explanation: A Venn diagram is just a sorting circle. Things that fit the rule go inside the circle; things that do not fit stay outside. Because the child has already been sorting into ‘something / not something’, a one-circle Venn diagram is a small, natural step: the circle is the ‘something’ pile, and outside is the ‘not something’ pile. How to teach it: Make a big circle on the floor or table with a loop of string. Say, ‘Inside this circle we will put all the animals.’ Give the child a mix of toy animals and other objects…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “This circle is for red things. Where does the red ball go?” Child places it inside. You: “Where does the blue cup go?” Child: “Outside – it is not red.” You: “Well done. Inside is for red…

concepts.title: Two Circles and the Middle (‘both’)
concepts.explanation: This is the newest and trickiest idea, so treat it as a gentle first taste rather than something to master. Make two circles that overlap with two loops of string. One circle is for ‘toys’, the other for ‘red things’. An object that is a toy and red – a red teddy – belongs in the middle, where the circles overlap, because it fits both rules at once. How to teach it: Start by placing objects that clearly fit only one circle – a blue car is a toy but not red, an apple is red but not a toy. Then bring out the red…
concepts.example: Thinking objects can only be sorted one way. The same pile can be sorted by colour, then by size, then by type. Re-sort the very same objects a different way to show this.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Unit 7 is the child's first taste of statistics – the maths of organising and comparing information. At this age that means three friendly ideas: sorting objects into groups called sets, comparing sets to say which has more or fewer, and using a simple Venn diagram (sorting circles) to show what belongs together. There are no big numbers here. The whole unit is about looking carefully, noticing what is the same and what is different, and explaining a sorting rule out loud.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Sort a group of objects into two sets using one rule (for example red / not red).

rules.text: Explain the sorting rule they used in their own words.

rounds.prompt: Here is some data: 2, 10, 13, 11, 18, 2. Which value appears most often (the mode)?
rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
rounds.explanation: 2 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

selfAssessment: I can sort a group of objects into two sets using one rule (for example red / not red).
I can explain the sorting rule they used in their own words.
I can understand that a set is a group of things that belong together.
I can count how many objects are in each set and answer how-many questions.
I can compare two sets and say which has more and which has fewer.
I can notice that the same objects can be sorted in different ways.
I can understand that a Venn diagram is a sorting circle: things that fit go inside, things that do not stay outside.
I can sort objects into a one-circle Venn diagram, and take first steps with two overlapping circles (the middle means both).
